Cowork is the perfect middle ground between the back and forth chatbot experience and the hardcore coding experience with AI. It has the same brain powering Claude Code, but in a much more easy-to-navigate window.

I personally have 3 rules of thumb for when to use Cowork over the regular chat mode… let me know if you have any others!

1. If you’re using a ton of documents. (You are probably pushing the context window and might be getting sub-par results in chat mode.)

2. If you’re using data and it’s super important that Claude doesn’t hallucinate. (Cowork writes and executes its own code which helps it to avoid hallucination.)

3. If you’re bouncing around between a lot of sources, especially on long tasks. (Cowork plans out its work before it gets started on big tasks, so it’s less likely to mess up or forget something.)

Pardon my French. Full list plus notes in the description —> What did I miss? Add your thoughts in the comments! 💬 

1️⃣(POSITIVE) Human creators have an easier time standing out against the flood of AI slop: 
• The audience wants to follow and support a creator they know is human 
• “Human-ness” becomes a brand asset 
• (But how do they know the creator is human… really?) 

2️⃣(NEGATIVE) Human creators will have a harder and harder time standing out: 
• Competing against AI slop that’s been engineered to flood the brain with dopamine 
• Fewer people will be creators as it’s no longer a viable way to make a living 

3️⃣(NEGATIVE) The internet will fill up with AI slop and fake news will outnumber real news 10,000:1: 
• This is bad for society because the internet is a useful source of information 
• It will be harder for people to access good information 
• It will be impossible to know what really happened and what didn’t 
• As a result, communities will become more insular and distrusting of outsiders 

4️⃣(POSITIVE) The negative effects of fake news will effectively go away: 
• Because if everyone mistrusts news on the internet, it won’t be treated as a source of any information 
• Fake news and real news alike will be disregarded 
• Positive side effect: ACTUAL fake news won’t be as easy to proliferate online 

5️⃣(NEUTRAL) The internet will become pointless and certain things will gain a premium: 
• In-person events (I cannot prove I’m real until you see me in the flesh) 
• Physical media (newspapers, magazines, things that are not a screen that took time and effort to produce) 
• Bonafide media curators (New York Times) 

6️⃣(NEUTRAL) Cognitive burn-out and nostalgia loops will take over: 
• People experience “epistemic fatigue” from constantly questioning what’s real 
• Users retreat to pre-AI nostalgia content (old YouTube, DVDs, VHS aesthetics) because it feels safer and familiar 
• Platforms lean into nostalgia because it lowers anxiety and sustains engagement 

Share your thoughtful debate in the comments! #ai ##aiandsociety

“Multiplayer AI” refers to when you and your other human collaborators are all working with AI at the same time— rather than going off in siloed conversations with ChatGPT, Claude, etc, and hacking the pieces back together later.

Most tools only support “single player mode” today, but teams that prioritize getting into “multiplayer” tools today will have a MAJOR advantage in the next 6-12 months.

@mirohq is the platform that defined remote collaboration during the pandemic (and before), so it makes total sense that they’re now owning the multiplayer AI experience as well.

With tools like Sidekicks (AI agents that can work directly on the board) and Flows (automations that connect your ideas and do busywork for you, also on the board), they’re leading the way in the multiplayer AI movement.

The fancy word is “temporal disintegration”. Fun fact: This phenomenon is also why you can clearly pinpoint when events happened that were close to the time you finished high school (completely different environments during and after school) but might struggle to remember what year major events happened during Covid lockdown. 

The first step to spending less time on your phone is to understand why it’s so damn hard to do in the first place! I know it’s ironic, but if you follow this page, you’ll learn more without even trying, I promise 😇

Full process below!👇 Save this for later 🌸

Honestly so many AI tool use cases make no sense, but this one immediately clicked with me. You know I’m only going to promote something to you guys if I think it’s actually got real potential to help you upgrade your life, so I’m stoked to share this amazing new use case solved by @mirohq with you :)

Step by step:

1. Open a transcription tool like ChatGPT, CapCut, or Wispr.
2. Describe in detail a process you want to train someone on / document. Be sure to mention key stakeholders, decision points, and tools.
3. Go to Miro, open a new board (or an existing one) and press the sparkle icon.
4. Select “Diagram or Mindmap”
5. Select “Flowchart”
6. Paste your transcript into the text box and press enter
7. When the flowchart is done, click the blue “Add to Canvas” button
8. Edit the flowchart for accuracy and design

AI isn’t making you dumber. How you’re USING AI is making you dumber. 

Every few days I see another post saying “AI is making people lazy” or “it’s killing our attention spans.”

But that is just the surface level take and ignores the people who are using it every day as an extension of their curiosity. Not to avoid thinking, but to think faster, deeper, and wider.

They ask things like:
🧠 “Walk me through how antibiotics actually work.”
⚡ “My prototype isn’t turning on — I have zero background in electrical engineering. What are possible failure points?”
🎧 “How do I generate two sound frequencies, 350 Hz and 440 Hz, in Audacity?”

These aren’t prompts to get an answer.
They’re prompts to understand the world.

Because the most powerful thing about AI is that it collapses the distance between wanting to know something and knowing it.
The gap between you and the “smartest people you know” isn’t intelligence.
It’s belief.
It’s the quiet decision to keep learning, even when it’s easier not to.

AI can’t make you dumber.
But it can expose whether you actually want to learn.

We are generally evaluating the value of AI’s impact on the economy the wrong way. 

It’s like the internet being invented and people focusing on “how is this going to reduce the cost of my grocery store?” 

Did the existence of the internet reduce costs across grocery store operations? 

Yes. 

Is that where the CORE VALUE of the internet showed up? 

No. 

The core value of internet showed up in search engines, streaming media, social networks, online payments, instant messaging, and so many other technologies that would have been *literally inconceivable* beforehand. 

We’re in that exact same moment in AI. 

So my take is— yes, we are in a bubble because Silicon Valley is basically one big AI circle jerk of funding right now. But no— we are not overvaluing AI’s future impact on our lives. If anything, I think we are still grossly UNDERestimating it.
